claims.claim_text: Developers who master AI coding assistants are 3-10x more productive.
claims.span_hint: section: What You'll Be Able to Do | Why this matters
claims.anchor_text: Developers who master them are 3-10x more productive.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Developers who use them well can see substantial productivity gains, though the exact gain varies by task and team.
claims.rationale: This is a precise productivity multiplier without a directly supporting allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: The module covers 13 major tools with verified 2025 pricing.
claims.span_hint: section: What You'll Be Able to Do | 2025 Update
claims.anchor_text: This module now covers **13 major tools** with verified 2025 pricing.
claims.suggested_rewrite: This module surveys a broad set of major AI coding tools, but pricing and plan details should be checked against current vendor pages.
claims.rationale: Multiple pricing claims in the module are stale relative to current vendor documentation, so the verification claim cannot stand as written.

claims.claim_text: Using AI assistants in the auth example saves 75% of the time.
claims.span_hint: section: The Productivity Revolution | paragraph after auth example
claims.anchor_text: **The difference**: 8 hours of grinding vs 2 hours of directing. 75% time saved.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **The difference**: less time spent typing and more time spent directing, reviewing, and integrating AI-generated work.
claims.rationale: This is a teaching statistic presented as fact without a source.

claims.claim_text: 55% of professional developers use AI coding assistants daily.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Hidden Impact of AI Coding
claims.anchor_text: **55% of professional developers** use AI coding assistants daily
claims.suggested_rewrite: Many professional developers now use AI coding assistants regularly.
claims.rationale: This exact percentage needs a specific study citation that is not provided here.

claims.claim_text: 40% of new code at companies using Copilot is AI-generated.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Hidden Impact of AI Coding
claims.anchor_text: **40% of new code** at companies using Copilot is AI-generated
claims.suggested_rewrite: Organizations using AI coding tools often report that a meaningful share of new code is AI-assisted.
claims.rationale: The exact percentage requires a pinned source and should not remain as an uncited number.

claims.claim_text: Knowing how to direct AI is the number one skill gap.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Hidden Impact of AI Coding
claims.anchor_text: **#1 skill gap**: Knowing HOW to direct AI, not IF to use it
claims.suggested_rewrite: A major skill shift is learning how to direct and verify AI effectively, not whether to try it at all.
claims.rationale: A ranked 'number one' claim needs explicit survey support.

claims.claim_text: Autocomplete tools make boilerplate coding 30-50% faster.
claims.span_hint: section: The Three Waves of AI Coding | Wave 1
claims.anchor_text: **Impact**: 30-50% faster coding for boilerplate
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Impact**: noticeably faster work on boilerplate and repetitive code.
claims.rationale: This is a precise performance range without a directly supporting allowlisted source.

claims.claim_text: Chat-and-code tools make new features and debugging 2-5x faster.
claims.span_hint: section: The Three Waves of AI Coding | Wave 2
claims.anchor_text: **Impact**: 2-5x faster for new features, debugging
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Impact**: often much faster for new features, debugging, and exploratory implementation.
claims.rationale: The exact multiplier range is uncited.

claims.claim_text: Agentic AI tools make refactoring, migrations, and complex tasks 5-10x faster.
claims.span_hint: section: The Three Waves of AI Coding | Wave 3
claims.anchor_text: **Impact**: 5-10x faster for refactoring, migrations, complex tasks
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Impact**: can dramatically speed up refactoring, migrations, and other multi-step tasks.
claims.rationale: The exact multiplier is presented as fact without a source.

claims.claim_text: GitHub Copilot offers official plans including Pro at $10/month, Business at $19 per seat per month, and free access for eligible students and some educators or maintainers.
claims.span_hint: section: GitHub Copilot | Pricing
claims.rationale: GitHub's plans page directly documents Copilot pricing and eligibility for free access.

claims.claim_text: Tabnine supports private deployment options including SaaS, VPC, on-prem, or air-gapped environments and advertises zero code retention plus compliance claims such as GDPR and SOC 2.
claims.span_hint: section: Tabnine | The Good
claims.rationale: These are real vendor capability and compliance claims, but the primary source is on an off-allowlist domain.

claims.claim_text: Tabnine has a free tier and a $12/month Pro plan.
claims.span_hint: section: Tabnine | Pricing
claims.anchor_text: **Pricing**: Free tier, $12/month Pro, custom Enterprise pricing
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Pricing**: Tabnine offers paid plans, but current packaging and pricing should be checked on the vendor site.
claims.rationale: Current Tabnine pricing does not match the plan structure stated here.

claims.claim_text: Codeium is completely free for individuals with unlimited usage and charges $12 per user per month for teams.
claims.span_hint: section: Codeium | The Good and Pricing
claims.anchor_text: **Pricing**: Free for individuals, $12/user/month for teams
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Pricing**: Codeium positioned itself around a free individual tier, but current plan names and prices should be checked on the vendor site.
claims.rationale: The exact pricing is stale and the surrounding product positioning has shifted.

claims.claim_text: Cursor offers Hobby, Pro, Pro+, Ultra, and Teams plans, with current listed prices including Pro at $20/month, Ultra at $200/month, and Teams at $40 per user per month.
claims.span_hint: section: Cursor | Pricing
claims.rationale: Cursor's current pricing is real and vendor-published, but the official domain is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Cursor replaced request caps with a usage credit pool system in June 2025.
claims.span_hint: section: Cursor | Recent Changes (2025)
claims.anchor_text: June 2025: Replaced request caps with usage credit pool system
claims.suggested_rewrite: Cursor's pricing and usage model has changed over time.
claims.rationale: This is a dated historical pricing claim that requires a specific vendor changelog and should not remain uncited.

claims.claim_text: Cursor's Auto mode began contributing to monthly usage in August 2025.
claims.span_hint: section: Cursor | Recent Changes (2025)
claims.anchor_text: August 2025: Auto (agentic coding) now contributes to monthly usage
claims.suggested_rewrite: Usage accounting for Cursor's agentic features has changed over time.
claims.rationale: This is another dated billing-history claim without a pinned source.

claims.claim_text: Windsurf's Cascade provides agentic coding features such as write/chat modes, tool access, and auto-run modes.
claims.span_hint: section: Windsurf and MCP matrix references
claims.rationale: These are real product capabilities, but the primary documentation host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Windsurf Pro costs $10/month.
claims.span_hint: section: Windsurf | Pricing
claims.anchor_text: **Pricing**: Free tier (limited usage), $10/month Pro
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Pricing**: Windsurf offers a free tier and paid plans, but the current prices should be checked on the vendor site.
claims.rationale: The exact Pro price has changed.

claims.claim_text: OpenAI says Codex is included with ChatGPT Plus, Pro, Business, Edu, and Enterprise plans, and can be used in the CLI, IDE extension, web, and app.
claims.span_hint: section: OpenAI Codex CLI | What it is and Pricing
claims.rationale: OpenAI's Codex plan article directly documents plan inclusion and supported surfaces.

claims.claim_text: OpenAI Codex CLI launched in October 2025.
claims.span_hint: section: OpenAI Codex CLI | What it is
claims.anchor_text: Official OpenAI terminal coding agent (launched October 2025)
claims.suggested_rewrite: Official OpenAI coding agent available through the CLI, IDE extensions, web, and app.
claims.rationale: OpenAI's own product announcements show Codex availability before October 2025, so this date is wrong.

claims.claim_text: OpenAI says GPT-5.1-Codex-Max is built for long-running work across multiple context windows over millions of tokens, and reports that 95% of OpenAI engineers use Codex weekly and ship 70% more pull requests after adopting it.
claims.span_hint: section: OpenAI Codex CLI | The Good and Real-world impact
claims.rationale: OpenAI's GPT-5.1-Codex-Max launch page directly states these capability and internal-usage claims.

claims.claim_text: Gemini CLI is an open-source terminal AI agent with a free tier offering 60 requests per minute, 1,000 requests per day, a 1M-token context window, and MCP support.
claims.span_hint: section: Google Gemini CLI | The Good and Pricing
claims.rationale: The official GitHub repository README lists the free-tier limits, context window, open-source status, and MCP support.

claims.claim_text: Gemini CLI had 70,000+ GitHub stars and 2,800+ community pull requests.
claims.span_hint: section: Google Gemini CLI | The Good
claims.anchor_text: Open source (70,000+ GitHub stars, 2,800+ community PRs)
claims.suggested_rewrite: Open source, with a large and active GitHub community.
claims.rationale: Live community metrics change over time and should not remain as uncited frozen numbers.

claims.claim_text: Aider is terminal-based, editor-agnostic, and tightly integrated with git, including automatically committing AI edits.
claims.span_hint: section: Aider.ai | What it is and The Good
claims.rationale: Aider's docs directly support these claims, but aider.chat is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Cline can create and edit files, execute terminal commands, use a browser, and extend itself with MCP tools under user approval.
claims.span_hint: section: Cline | The Good and MCP references
claims.rationale: The official Cline README describes file editing, command execution, browser use, and MCP extensibility.

claims.claim_text: Goose is from Block (formerly Square).
claims.span_hint: section: Goose by Block (Square)
claims.anchor_text: Open-source AI agent for full software development automation from Block (formerly Square)
claims.suggested_rewrite: Open-source AI agent for software development automation.
claims.rationale: The public repository now indicates a move to the Agentic AI Foundation, so the attribution is stale.

claims.claim_text: Open Interpreter runs code locally in Python, JavaScript, shell, and other languages, and asks for confirmation before execution by default.
claims.span_hint: section: Open Interpreter | The Good
claims.rationale: The official repository README documents local code execution across languages and the confirmation behavior.

claims.claim_text: Within three months of MCP's announcement, 500+ MCP servers had been created by the community.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The MCP Origin Story
claims.anchor_text: **500+ MCP servers** were created by the community
claims.suggested_rewrite: A large ecosystem of community MCP servers emerged quickly after the protocol was introduced.
claims.rationale: This exact count is a moving ecosystem metric and is not directly pinned to an allowlisted source here.

claims.claim_text: All major AI coding tools added MCP support within three months.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The MCP Origin Story
claims.anchor_text: **All major AI coding tools** added MCP support
claims.suggested_rewrite: MCP support spread quickly across the AI tooling ecosystem.
claims.rationale: This universal market-wide claim is too broad to keep without precise sourcing.

claims.claim_text: Anthropic engineers noticed that 80% of AI coding tool limitations came from context access rather than model capability.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The MCP Origin Story
claims.anchor_text: Anthropic engineers noticed that 80% of AI coding tool limitations came from **context access**, not model capability.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A major limitation of many AI coding tools is access to the right context, not just raw model quality.
claims.rationale: The exact 80% figure is uncited and should be removed.

claims.claim_text: Claude Pro does not include Claude Code CLI access.
claims.span_hint: section: Anthropic Claude Code CLI | The Not-So-Good
claims.anchor_text: ️ **NOT included with Claude Pro subscription** ($20/month web chat subscription does NOT give you API access!)
claims.suggested_rewrite: Claude Code access and Anthropic API billing should be checked against Anthropic's current plan documentation, because subscription and API entitlements are distinct and have changed over time.
claims.rationale: Anthropic's current pricing page says Claude Pro includes Claude Code access, so this statement is outdated.

claims.claim_text: Claude Code would cost about $3-5 for the entire curriculum.
claims.span_hint: section: Anthropic Claude Code CLI | Pricing
claims.anchor_text: **~$3-5 for this entire Neural Dojo curriculum**
claims.suggested_rewrite: Actual Claude Code cost depends on model choice, context size, and how heavily you use the tool.
claims.rationale: This is a usage-cost estimate presented as a fact without a stable source.

claims.claim_text: Continue is an open-source AI coding assistant for VS Code and JetBrains with chat, autocomplete, edit, and agent workflows, and it supports MCP servers.
claims.span_hint: section: Continue.dev and MCP sections
claims.rationale: Continue's docs directly support these claims, but continue.dev is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: ChatGPT Pro is specifically priced for O1 access.
claims.span_hint: section: ChatGPT | Pricing
claims.anchor_text: **Pricing**: Free tier or $20/month Plus ($200/month Pro for O1 access)
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Pricing**: Free, Plus, and Pro plans are available; check OpenAI's current pricing page for exact plan features.
claims.rationale: The model-specific framing is stale relative to current OpenAI plan descriptions.

claims.claim_text: Gemini Advanced provides a 2 million token context window in its Pro tier.
claims.span_hint: section: Gemini Advanced | The Good
claims.anchor_text: Long context window (2M tokens in Pro!)
claims.suggested_rewrite: Long-context capabilities are a major part of Gemini's positioning, but the exact limits depend on Google's current plan offerings.
claims.rationale: Current Google consumer plan documentation does not match this exact claim.

claims.claim_text: Gemini Advanced costs $20/month.
claims.span_hint: section: Gemini Advanced | Pricing
claims.anchor_text: **Pricing**: Free tier, $20/month for Advanced
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Pricing**: Google offers free and paid Gemini consumer plans, but the current plan names and prices should be checked on Google's site.
claims.rationale: Google's consumer AI plan naming and packaging have changed, so this line is stale.

claims.claim_text: 90% of beginners get subscriptions-vs-API access wrong.
claims.span_hint: section: The #1 Confusion in AI Coding
claims.anchor_text: ### The #1 Confusion in AI Coding (90% of Beginners Get This Wrong!)
claims.suggested_rewrite: ### A Common Confusion in AI Coding
claims.rationale: The '90%' figure is an uncited rhetorical statistic.

claims.claim_text: ChatGPT Plus gives unlimited web chat plus Codex CLI.
claims.span_hint: section: Chat Subscriptions table
claims.anchor_text: | **ChatGPT Plus** | $20/mo | Unlimited web chat + **Codex CLI** |  No (except Codex CLI) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **ChatGPT Plus** | $20/mo | Paid ChatGPT access with Codex availability governed by current plan limits |  No (except where an included OpenAI coding tool is explicitly provided) |
claims.rationale: The 'unlimited web chat' wording is not stable and the exact entitlement wording should track current plan documentation.

claims.claim_text: Claude Pro is web-only and cannot be used with Claude Code.
claims.span_hint: section: Chat Subscriptions table
claims.anchor_text: | **Claude Pro** | $20/mo | 5× more web chat usage |  No - web only |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Claude Pro** | $20/mo | Paid Claude app access; current Claude Code and API entitlements should be checked in Anthropic's latest plan docs |  Not the same thing as standalone API billing |
claims.rationale: Anthropic's current pricing no longer matches the simple 'web only' framing.

claims.claim_text: Gemini Advanced is a $20/month web-only subscription with Google Workspace access.
claims.span_hint: section: Chat Subscriptions table
claims.anchor_text: | **Gemini Advanced** | $20/mo | Web chat + Google Workspace |  No - web only |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Google's Gemini consumer plan** |  | Consumer Gemini access with feature limits and integrations defined by Google's current plan docs |  Separate from API credentials used by external coding tools |
claims.rationale: Google's consumer AI plan naming and packaging have changed enough that this row should not remain frozen as written.

further_reading.title: Introducing the Model Context Protocol
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for what MCP is, when Anthropic introduced it, and why it matters for tool-connected AI.

further_reading.title: GitHub Copilot Plans
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for current Copilot plan structure, pricing, and eligibility details.

further_reading.title: Using Codex with Your ChatGPT Plan
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary OpenAI reference for which ChatGPT plans include Codex and where Codex can be used.

further_reading.title: Gemini CLI Repository
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for Gemini CLI's open-source status, free-tier limits, MCP support, and current community footprint.

notes: Skipped pure analogies, quiz items, and fenced code examples. Grouped adjacent vendor-capability bullets when one authoritative source could support the block. Several pricing, plan-boundary, and dated-product claims in this module are stale as of April 21, 2026, so they were marked for rewrite rather than force-cited.
